<insights>

- High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
- Perfect cohesion (1.0) and zero coupling confirm this is a tightly scoped, self-contained test zone with no production dependencies leaking in.
- boundary-check.test.ts is a critical architectural guard — it enforces the web-shared foundation contract and the viewer/server boundary; treat failures here as blocking.
- Only two files cover integration concerns for a 349-file package (Web Application Core); consider whether additional integration scenarios (e.g. server route wiring, MCP handler contracts) deserve coverage here.
- boundary-check.test.ts enforces the web-shared zero-upward-dependency rule — this test should be guarded from skipping or removal as strongly as domain-isolation.test.js in the e2e suite.
- Two integration test files for a large package is lean; as the web package grows, this zone should expand to cover server-viewer contract boundaries, not just build output shape.
- Zone contains only test files, making the '-tests' suffix appropriate and ensuring tooling correctly classifies this as a test-only zone.
- web-integration-tests and e2e-tests form a complementary two-layer guard with no overlap: web-integration-tests enforces intra-package boundaries (web-shared zero-upward-dependency, viewer/server build contract) while e2e-tests enforces inter-package tier isolation (spawn-only, gateway contracts, domain isolation) — new architectural policies should be routed to the correct layer based on scope.
- boundary-check.test.ts here and architecture-policy.test.js in e2e-tests guard complementary scopes without duplication — this clean division of intra-package vs cross-package responsibility is a structural quality signal that should be preserved when adding new enforcement rules.
- web-integration-tests has no test covering the viewer-server type contract: the 37 web-viewer→web-server import edges involve types that must be compatible across the build boundary, but boundary-check.test.ts only verifies zero-upward-dependency for web-shared and does not assert on the viewer/server type surface.
- build-output-contract.test.ts validates build output shape but does not assert on the content of the emitted index.html (e.g. expected script/link tags, absence of external URLs) — the static-assets zone's no-CDN invariant has no automated integration-layer guard.
- No integration test asserts on the viewer/server type contract: the 37 web-viewer→web-server imports consume server-side types that could silently drift between the two build targets — a type-compatibility assertion in boundary-check.test.ts (e.g. verifying that all viewer-imported server types are re-exported through a gateway) would close this gap.
- boundary-check.test.ts has grown to 307 lines covering 7 distinct test cases: server→viewer boundary, viewer gateway compliance (external.ts), sub-zone barrel enforcement (crash/), shared/ zero-upward-dependency, crash-detector.ts positive import assertion, panel component barrel enforcement, and viewer→server reverse boundary. The zone's previously noted '14 internal calls' understates actual test breadth — the file implements two shared helper functions (collectTsFiles, extractImportPaths) called by all 7 tests, accounting for the internal call density.
- The boundary-check.test.ts test at line 207 ('crash-detector.ts imports ViewId from shared/, not external.ts') is a positive assertion that enforces the isCrash exemption — it does not merely permit the direct import but requires it. This converts the previous finding that called the direct shared/ import 'a minor gateway compliance gap' into a documented design requirement with mechanical enforcement. The exemption and its test together protect against a future cycle through performance/index.ts.
- boundary-check.test.ts enforces crash-detector.ts's direct shared/ import (line 207) but does not enforce that performance/index.ts's crash re-exports are the only secondary path for crash types. A consumer that bypasses crash/index.js by importing from performance/index.js is undetected by any current test — the dual import path for crash types (crash/index.js and performance/index.js) has no coverage parity check.
- boundary-check.test.ts does not assert that crash zone consumers import from crash/index.js directly rather than from the performance/index.js barrel that re-exports crash's entire public API. crash-recovery-banner.ts currently imports SavedNavigationState from performance/index.js, fragmenting crash-type consumers across two import paths. Adding a check that production files outside viewer/crash/ and viewer/performance/ do not import crash-originating types via performance/index.js would enforce import-path consistency for the crash zone.
- [call graph] 14 internal calls, 0 outgoing, 0 incoming (cohesion: 1, coupling: 0)

</insights>
